title: 1Password vs Enpass for Minimalists
meta_description: 1Password vs Enpass for minimalists who want local password control. See why Enpass wins when avoiding hosted vault services.
constraint_lens: Minimalists prefer tools that avoid extra services and keep password storage directly under their own control.

one_second_verdict.summary: Best for minimalists who want passwords stored in a locally controlled database.
one_second_verdict.reason: 1Password fails first because credentials must be stored inside a vendor hosted vault instead of a local database.

verdict.summary: Enpass is the better choice for minimalists who want full control over where password data lives. It stores credentials in a local encrypted database that remains on the user's device and can optionally sync through storage services chosen by the user. 1Password requires creating an account and storing credentials inside the vendor hosted vault. For users who want to avoid third party vault storage entirely, that hosted model introduces a dependency they do not want.
verdict.decision_rule: If the password manager requires storing credentials inside a vendor-hosted vault instead of a locally controlled database, 1Password fails first.

sections.heading: Why Enpass fits minimalists
sections.content: The user refuses to keep personal credentials inside a hosted vault service and wants the password database under their own control. Enpass stores passwords in a local encrypted vault file that remains on the device. If synchronization is needed, the user connects the vault to storage services they already control. This approach avoids storing passwords inside a vendor managed vault.

bullets.point: Enpass stores passwords in a local encrypted database on the user's device.
bullets.why_it_matters: Minimalists can keep full control over where password data is stored.

bullets.point: Enpass does not require creating a vendor account to access the vault.
bullets.why_it_matters: Users avoid maintaining another online account just to manage passwords.

bullets.point: Enpass allows syncing the vault through external storage services chosen by the user.
bullets.why_it_matters: The database remains under the user's control instead of being locked into a proprietary vault.

bullets.point: 1Password automatically synchronizes the password vault across devices through the account.
bullets.why_it_matters: Users can access credentials instantly across laptops, phones, and browsers.

bullets.point: 1Password browser extensions detect login forms and autofill credentials.
bullets.why_it_matters: Signing into websites becomes faster without copying passwords manually.

bullets.point: 1Password restores the password vault when the user signs into a new device.
bullets.why_it_matters: Credentials become available automatically after logging into the account.

items.fails_when: The user wants passwords to sync instantly across devices without configuring external storage connections.
items.what_to_do_instead: Use 1Password where synchronization happens automatically through the account.

items.fails_when: The user refuses to store credentials in a hosted vault because 1Password requires using the vendor managed vault service.
items.what_to_do_instead: Use Enpass where passwords remain in a locally controlled database.

sections.heading: When this verdict might flip
sections.content: If the user prefers automatic synchronization across all devices without configuring storage connections, 1Password may become the better option.

sections.heading: Quick decision rules
rules: Pick Enpass if you want full control over a local password database.
Pick Enpass if you refuse to store passwords in a hosted vault service.
Pick 1Password if you want automatic syncing and autofill across devices.

faqs.a: Enpass stores passwords in a local encrypted database and does not require using a hosted vault service.
No. 1Password stores credentials inside a hosted vault connected to the user account.

faqs.q: Can Enpass work without internet access?
faqs.a: Yes. Enpass runs locally and does not require an online service.
Users who want passwords to sync automatically across devices may prefer 1Password.
